items.correct: A sustained rise in the general price level over time — reducing the purchasing power of money. Measured by the percentage change in a price index.
items.misconception: Inflation is when prices are high.
items.random_wrong: Inflation is when the economy grows too fast.
items.why: Inflation is about RISING prices, not high prices. A country can have stable high prices (no inflation) or rising low prices (high inflation). Students confuse level with rate of change.

items.q: What is the difference between a shift in demand and a movement along the demand curve?
items.correct: A movement along the curve is caused by a change in price; a shift of the whole curve is caused by changes in other factors (income, tastes, substitute prices).
items.misconception: A change in price shifts the demand curve.
items.random_wrong: Demand always falls when supply increases.
items.why: This is the single most common error in introductory economics. Price changes cause movements ALONG the curve; the curve itself shifts only when non-price factors change.

items.q: What is the difference between revenue and profit?
items.correct: Revenue is total income from sales (price x quantity); profit is what remains after deducting costs from revenue.
items.misconception: Revenue and profit mean the same thing.
items.random_wrong: Revenue is profit before tax and profit is revenue after tax.
items.why: A business with high revenue can still make a loss if costs exceed it. Revenue = sales income; profit = revenue minus costs. Conflating them gives nonsensical analysis.

items.q: Which famous example is associated with hyperinflation in the 1920s?
items.why: It's Weimar Germany. “Japan” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Inflation FALLING but still positive is called?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Disinflation. “Deflation” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: CPI rises from 200 to 220. Inflation rate (%)?
items.why: Exact answer: 10. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
